summary: Turing wrote in a distinct register across three modes: (1) formal mathematical logic, (2) semi-formal philosophical prose, and (3) practical technical prose. His style across all three is characterized by economy, directness, and precision — he rarely uses unnecessary ornamentation, favors short declarative sentences for punch, and lets the weight of his ideas carry the text rather than rhetorical flourish.

sentence_complexity.average_sentence_length: Short to medium. In mathematical papers: compact, definition-theorem-proof structure. In correspondence: short and direct.
sentence_complexity.paragraph_structure: Turing used tight, focused paragraphs in technical work. In philosophical prose, he allowed more discursive movement between ideas.
preferred_constructs: Conditional 'if-then' structures (central to both his mathematical and philosophical prose)
Nominalization of processes (e.g., 'computability,' 'decidability') — turning operations into objects of analysis
Active voice in correspondence and practical prose
Passive constructions in theorem-proof exposition (conventional mathematical register)
avoided_constructs: Excessive hedging — Turing states results firmly
Appeal to authority without logical justification

vocabulary_profile.technical_density: Very high when writing on computability, logic, cryptanalysis. Turing was capable of dropping into plain English when explaining to non-specialists.
vocabulary_profile.register_flexibility: Remarkably fluid — he could write a dense logical proof and then, in correspondence, use plain direct English like 'I am sure I could not have found anywhere another companion so brilliant'
vocabulary_profile.foreign_language_use: Latin/Greek mathematical terms (logicism tradition); references to ordinary language in philosophical discussions
distinctive_word_choices: machinery (used deliberately in 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence')

rhetorical_patterns.pattern: Definition-first, then problem statement, then proof/discussion
rhetorical_patterns.occurs_in: On Computable Numbers, Systems of Logic Based on Ordinals
rhetorical_patterns.example: Turing opens with the concept of a 'computing machine' and builds the entire edifice from that single primitive

rhetorical_patterns.pattern: Question posing — reframes a loaded question into an empirically testable form
rhetorical_patterns.example: Turing opens with 'I propose to consider the question, Can machines think?' — immediately reframing it as an imitation game

rhetorical_patterns.pattern: Concrete analogy to introduce abstract mechanism
rhetorical_patterns.occurs_in: On Computable Numbers — uses the child's exercise book marked off in squares as the starting point for the Turing machine
rhetorical_patterns.example: Turing did not begin from any existing computing machine — he began from a child's notebook

rhetorical_patterns.pattern: Modest disclaimer before a bold claim
rhetorical_patterns.example: Turing anticipates objections (Divine Spirit objection, Mathematical Objection, Arguments from Various Disabilities) and addresses them in order — showing he considered counterarguments before making his case

cited_quotes_with_context.quote: I propose to consider the question, 'Can machines think?'
cited_quotes_with_context.style_note: Direct, declarative, question-framing opening — immediately establishes the paper's empirical orientation

cited_quotes_with_context.quote: We may hope that machines will eventually compete with men in all purely intellectual fields.
cited_quotes_with_context.style_note: Qualified hope ('may hope') followed by bold scope claim — Turing hedged slightly but the claim itself is sweeping

cited_quotes_with_context.quote: The development of these ideas leads to the author's definition of a computable function, and to an identification of computability with effective calculability.
cited_quotes_with_context.style_note: Turing uses 'the author's' — formal academic register, conventional for the time; the prose is dry but the ideas are revolutionary
